section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE

nearby_text: Two cases of special interest are when A is additive $ (k = 0) $, yielding $$ E[\Delta_{i}(\infty)]=2a\left[u_{i}(p_{0})-p_{0}\right] $$ (26.11b) and when A is recessive $ (k = -1) $, in which case $$ E[\Delta_{i}(\infty)]=2a\left[u_{i}(p_{0})-p_{0}^{2}\right] $$ (26.11c)

label: Formula 26.12
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Basic Theory
nearby_text: The variance (and indeed all higher moments) of the total response at the selection limit are easily computed, as, regardless of the value of $ k $, $ \Delta_{i}(\infty) $ takes on only two values, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta_{i}(\infty)&=2a-m_{i}(p_{0})\quad with probability u_{i}(p_{0})\\&=\quad0-m_{i}(p_{0})\quad with probability1-u_{i}(p_{0})\end{aligned} $$ (26.12)

label: Formula 26.14b
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: $ is the initial additive variance contributed by the locus. Because we assumed no epistasis and no linkage disequilibrium, summing over all loci gives the cumulative response at generation $ t $ as $$ R(t)\simeq2N_{e}\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right)\frac{S\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}} $$ (26.14b)

label: Formula 26.15b
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: ided that the conditions for the breeder's equation hold. Equation 26.14b implies that $$ R(t)\simeq2N_{e}\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right)R(1) $$ (26.15a) returning an expected limiting total response of $$ R(\infty)\simeq2N_{e}R(1) $$ (26.15b) Because $  R(1)/\sigma_z = h^2 S/\sigma_z = h^2 \bar{\tau}  $, the expected limiting response in terms of phenotypic standard deviations is $$ R(\infty)/\sigma_{z}\simeq h^{2}(2N_{e}\bar{\imath}) $$

label: Formula 26.15d
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: pster (1955b) and formally derived by Robertson (1960a). An alternative derivation of Equation 26.15a is as follows. Assuming the main force for allele-frequency change is drift, Equation 11.2 yields $$ \sigma_{A}^{2}(t)\simeq\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)[1-1/(2N_{e})]^{t}\simeq\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)\exp\left[-t/(2N_{e})\right] $$ (26.15d)

label: Formula 26.16a
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: $ \Delta_i(\infty) = 2N_e\,2a^2p_0(1 - p_0)S/\sigma_z^2 $. Substituting $ S/\sigma_z^2 = \bar{\imath}/\sigma_z $ yields the ratio of maximum to expected contribution as $$ \frac{\Delta_{i}^{max}}{\Delta_{i}(\infty)}=\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\frac{2a(1-p_{0})}{2a^{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})\bar{\imath}/\sigma_{z}}=\frac{\sigma_{z}}{2N_{e}\bar{\imath}a p_{0}} $$ (26.16a)

  "nearby_text": "$ \\Delta_i(\\infty) = 2N_e\\,2a^2p_0(1 - p_0)S/\\sigma_z^2 $. Substituting $ S/\\sigma_z^2 = \\bar{\\imath}/\\sigma_z $ yields the ratio of maximum to expected contribution as $$ \\frac{\\Delta_{i}^{max}}{\\Delta_{i}(\\infty)}=\\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\\frac{2a(1-p_{0})}{2a^{2}p_{0}(1-p_{0})\\bar{\\imath}/\\sigma_{z}}=\\frac{\\sigma_{z}}{2N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}a p_{0}} $$ (26.16a)"
}

label: Formula 26.16b
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: Thus, $ 2N_e R(1) $ overestimates the ultimate limit $ (\Delta_i(\infty) > \Delta_i^{max}) $ when $$ 2N_{e}\bar{\imath}a p_{0}>\sigma_{z} $$ (26.16b) implying that $ 2N_{e}R(1) $ overestimates the ultimate limit when this inequality is satisfied.

        "section": "DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE",

label: Formula 26.17
section: DRIFT AND LONG-TERM SELECTION RESPONSE
subsection: Robertson's Theory of Selection Limits
nearby_text: pected half-life of response, $ t_{0.5} $, the time required to obtain half the final response. Recalling Equation 26.14a, and solving $ 1 - e^{-t_{0.5}/2N_e} = 1/2 $, yields an expected half-life of $$ t_{0.5}=N_{e}\ln2\simeq1.4N_{e} $$ (26.17)

label: Formula 26.18
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: single chromosome). Assuming weak selection, Robertson (1970a) found that the ratio of the free-recombination limit to the complete-linkage limit (i.e., the best initial chromosome) is approximately $$ \frac{L_{f}}{L_{0}}\simeq1+\frac{2}{3}(N_{e}\bar{\imath}h^{*})^{2}\quad\mathrm{w h e n}\quad2N_{e}\bar{\imath}h^{*}<1 $$ (26.18)

Assuming weak selection, Robertson (1970a) found that the ratio of the free-recombination limit to the complete-linkage limit (i.e., the best initial chromosome) is approximately $$ \\frac{L_{f}}{L_{0}}\\simeq1+\\frac{2}{3}(N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}h^{*})^{2}\\quad\\mathrm{w h e n}\\quad2N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}h^{*}<1 $$ (26.18)"

label: Formula 26.19
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: rong, under free recombination all favored alleles will be fixed, and the total response becomes $ L_f = 2na(1 - p) $. Noting that $ a = \sigma_A^* / \sqrt{2np(1 - p)} $, this can also be restated as $$ L_{f}=2na(1-p)=\sigma_{A*}\sqrt{\frac{2n(1-p)}{p}} $$ (26.19)

  "nearby_text": "rong, under free recombination all favored alleles will be fixed, and the total response becomes $ L_f = 2na(1 - p) $. Noting that $ a = \\sigma_A^* / \\sqrt{2np(1 - p)} $, this can also be restated as $$ L_{f}=2na(1-p)=\\sigma_{A*}\\sqrt{\\frac{2n(1-p)}{p}} $$ (26.19)"
}

label: Formula 26.20a
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: expressed in terms of standard deviations (here $ \sigma_{A*} $) above the mean, so that if $ x_{2N} $ is the standardized largest order statistic in a sample of 2N chromosomes, the limit is given by $$ L_{0}=(x_{2N}\sqrt{2})\sigma_{A*} $$ (26.20a)

label: Formula 26.20b
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: Robertson (1970a) showed, for $10 < N < 40$, that $x_{2N} \sqrt{2} \simeq 3$, so that $L_{0}/\sigma_{A}^{2} \simeq 3$. Hence, for these values of $N$, $$ \frac{L_{f}}{L_{0}}\simeq\frac{1}{3}\sqrt{\frac{2n(1-p)}{p}} $$ (26.20b)

  "nearby_text": "Robertson (1970a) showed, for $10 < N < 40$, that $x_{2N} \\sqrt{2} \\simeq 3$, so that $L_{0}/\\sigma_{A}^{2} \\simeq 3$. Hence, for these values of $N$, $$ \\frac{L_{f}}{L_{0}}\\simeq\\frac{1}{3}\\sqrt{\\frac{2n(1-p)}{p}} $$ (26.20b)"
}

label: Formula 26.20c
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: when N = 80 and to 4.6 when N = 500. For larger values of N, if we use the asymptotic approximation for the largest order statistic given by Kendall and Stuart (1977), the factor of 3 is replaced by $$ x_{2N}\sqrt{2}\simeq\frac{0.577}{\sqrt{\ln(2N)}}+2\sqrt{\ln(2N)} $$ (26.20c)

label: Formula 26.20d
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: hes a value independent of $n$ and $p$, namely, the infinitesimal limit, $L_f = 2N_e\bar{\imath}h^* \sigma_{A^*}$. Thus, with strong selection and a large number of loci, Equation 26.20a implies that $$ L_{f}/L_{0}\simeq\frac{2N_{e}\bar{\imath}h^{*}\sigma_{A*}}{\left(x_{2N}\sqrt{2}\right)\sigma_{A*}}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{x_{2N}}\right)N_{e}\bar{\imath}h^{*}\qquad\mathrm{w h e n}N_{e}\bar{\imath}h^{*}>5 $$ (26.20d)

  "nearby_text": "hes a value independent of $n$ and $p$, namely, the infinitesimal limit, $L_f = 2N_e\\bar{\\imath}h^* \\sigma_{A^*}$. Thus, with strong selection and a large number of loci, Equation 26.20a implies that $$ L_{f}/L_{0}\\simeq\\frac{2N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}h^{*}\\sigma_{A*}}{\\left(x_{2N}\\sqrt{2}\\right)\\sigma_{A*}}=\\left(\\frac{\\sqrt{2}}{x_{2N}}\\right)N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}h^{*}\\qquad\\mathrm{w h e n}N_{e}\\bar{\\imath}h^{*}>5 $$ (26.20d)"
}

label: Formula 26.20e
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: Robertson also observed that for $ N_e \bar{\imath} h^* > 5 $, the half-life with no recombination is $$ t_{0.5}\simeq\frac{2}{\bar{\imath}h^{*}} $$ (26.20e) generations, and that differences in response (relative to free recombination) only become apparent after this number of generations has passed.

label: Formula 26.21a
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: THE EFFECTS OF LINKAGE ON THE SELECTION LIMIT
nearby_text: Allowing for some recombination (at a rate of $ \sim\ell/n $ between loci), Robertson found that the limit for a chromosome of length $ \ell $ is $$ L_{\ell}/L_{0}\simeq1+(N_{e}\ell/3)\qquad\mathrm{w h e n}\ N_{e}\ell\ll1 $$ (26.21a)

section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: OPTIMAL SELECTION INTENSITIES FOR MAXIMIZING LONG-TERM RESPONSE
nearby_text: $, and with $U$ denoting a unit normal random variable and $\varphi(x)$ denotes the unit normal density function. Because the number saved is $N = M p$, we have (following Hospital and Chevalet 1993) $$ \begin{align*}R(t)&\simeq M p\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right)\frac{\varphi(x_{[1-p]})\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)}{p\sigma_{z}}\\&=\varphi(x_{[1-p]})\left[\frac{M\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)}{\sigma_{z}}\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right)\right]\end{align*} $$ (26.23) Because the term in brackets is independent of $p$, response (as a function of $p$) is maximized at the maximum value of $\varphi(x_{[1-p]})$, which occurs at $x = 0$, or a $p$ value of 0.5.   "nearby_text": "n selection on a locus is strong ( $ 2N_{e}s \\gg 1 $), the probability that a selected line formed from a bottlenecked base population will eventually become fixed for the favored allele converges to $$ u_{N_{0}}(p_{0})=1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}} $$ (26.25a) where $ p_0 $ is the major-allele frequency in the population being sampled. This follows because if selection is sufficiently strong, the favored allele will become fixed if it is found in the initi"
}

label: Formula 26.25b
section: EFFECTS OF POPULATION STRUCTURE ON LONG-TERM RESPONSE
subsection: Founder Effects and Population Bottlenecks
nearby_text: ility of $ 1 - (1 - p_0)^{2N_0} $. Using this approximation, the ratio of the expected limiting contribution from such a locus to the expected contribution when the founding population is infinite is $$ \frac{u_{N_{0}}(p_{0})-p_{0}}{u(p_{0})-p_{0}}\simeq\frac{1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}}-p_{0}}{1-p_{0}}=1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}-1}\simeq1-e^{-p_{o}(2N_{0}-1)} $$ (26.25b) A more accurate measure would be to weight the fixation probability, $ u(p) $, by the sampling probability given a starting allele frequency, $ \sum_{i=1}^{2N_0} \Pr(i \mid p_0, 2N_0) u(i / [2N_0]) $

Using this approximation, the ratio of the expected limiting contribution from such a locus to the expected contribution when the founding population is infinite is $$ \\frac{u_{N_{0}}(p_{0})-p_{0}}{u(p_{0})-p_{0}}\\simeq\\frac{1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}}-p_{0}}{1-p_{0}}=1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}-1}\\simeq1-e^{-p_{o}(2N_{0}-1)} $$ (26.25b) A more accurate measure would be to weight the fixation probability, $ u(p) $, by the sampling probability given a starting allele frequency, $ \\sum_{i=1}^{2N_0} \\Pr(i \\mid p_0, 2N_0) u(i / [2N_0]) $"
}

section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: We start by assuming complete additivity and ignore any effects of gametic-phase disequilibrium. From Equation 11.20b, the expected additive genetic variance at generation t is $$ \sigma_{A}^{2}(t)\simeq2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}+\left[\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)-2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right]\exp(-t/2N_{e}) $$ (26.27)

section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT

label: Formula 26.28b
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT

label: Formula 26.29
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: phenotypic variance, $ \sigma_z^2 $, does not significantly change over time and that any disequilibrium is ignored. For $ t \gg 2N_e $, the per-generation response approaches an asymptotic limit of $$ r_{m}(\infty)=2N_{e}\bar{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}} $$ (26.29)

label: Formula 26.30a
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: Summing Equation 26.28b over generations (using the approximation given by Equation 7.28b) yields a cumulative response due to new mutation of $$ R_{m}(t)=\sum_{\tau=1}^{t}r_{m}(\tau)\simeq2N_{e}\overline{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}}\bigg(t-2N_{e}[1-\exp(-t/2N_{e})]\bigg) $$ (26.30a) as found by Hill (1982a, 1990) and Weber and Diggins (1990). An approximation for genes of sufficiently large effect ( $ |a| \gg \sigma_z/N\bar{i} $) is to consider them as being essentially fixed in

label: Formula 26.30c
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: Combining the mutational response with the response due to genetic variation that was originally in the base population (Equation 26.15a) yields an expected cumulative response of $$ R(t)=2N_{e}\frac{\overline{\imath}}{\sigma_{z}}\left[t\sigma_{m}^{2}+\left(1-\exp(-t/2N_{e})\right)\left(\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)-2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right)\right] $$ (26.30c)

label: Formula 26.31c
section: Long-term Response: Introduction
subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: variance at the mutation-drift equilibrium exceeds the initial additive variance ( $ \sigma_A^2(0) \ll 2N_e \sigma_m^2 $), the approximation $ \ln(1 + x) \simeq x $ for small values of $ |x| $ yields $$ \begin{align*}t^*\simeq2N_e\Psi={h^2\over(1-h^2)h_m^2}\end{align*} $$ (26.31c)

label: Formula 26.32
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Expected Asymptotic Response Under More General Conditions
nearby_text: bility of fixation (the latter can be obtained by Equation 7.18a, using the fitnesses given by Equation 25.4). Because $ 2M\mu $ new mutants appear each generation, the asymptotic rate of response is $$ \begin{align*}r_{m}(\infty)&=2M\mu E\bigg[2\alpha\cdot u\left(\frac{1}{2M},\alpha,k\right)\bigg]\\&=2M\mu\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}2\alpha\cdot u\left(\frac{1}{2M},\alpha,k\right)f(\alpha,k)d\alpha d k\end{align*} $$ (26.32)

Because $ 2M\\mu $ new mutants appear each generation, the asymptotic rate of response is $$ \\begin{align*}r_{m}(\\infty)&=2M\\mu E\\bigg[2\\alpha\\cdot u\\left(\\frac{1}{2M},\\alpha,k\\right)\\bigg]\\\\&=2M\\mu\\int_{-\\infty}^{\\infty}\\int_{-\\infty}^{\\infty}2\\alpha\\cdot u\\left(\\frac{1}{2M},\\alpha,k\\right)f(\\alpha,k)d\\alpha d k\\end{align*} $$ (26.32)"

label: Formula 26.33a
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Expected Asymptotic Response Under More General Conditions
nearby_text: Consider first the case where all new mutants are additive $ (k = 0) $. Hill (1982b) found that, provided major alleles are not common among new mutants, $$ r_{m}(\infty)\simeq2N_{e}\bar{\imath}\mu\frac{E^{+}[\alpha^{2}]}{\sigma_{z}}=\frac{4N_{e}\bar{\imath}\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}}\frac{E^{+}[\alpha^{2}]}{E[\alpha^{2}]} $$ (26.33a) where $$ E^{+}[\alpha^{2}]=\int_{0}^{\infty}\alpha^{2}f(\alpha)d\alpha $$ (26.33b) is the average squared increment of favorable alleles (i.e., those with $ \alpha > 0 $). The last equality assumes the distribution of mutational values and rates to be the same at each locus. When all mutations are additive (k = 0) and symmetric (E[ ] = 0),"

section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Expected Asymptotic Response Under More General Conditions
nearby_text: are additive (k = 0) and symmetric (E[ ] = 0), this reduces to our previous definition of $ \sigma_m^2 $. More generally, with complete additivity, but removing the assumption that E[ ] = 0, we have $$ \sigma_{m}^{2}=2\mu E[\alpha^{2}] $$ (26.34b) while with complete dominance $ (k=1) $, $$ \sigma_{m}^{2}=2\mu E\big[\left(2\alpha\right)^{2}\big]=8\mu E\big[\alpha^{2}\big] $$ (26.34c)

label: Formula 26.34c

label: Formula 26.36a
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Expected Asymptotic Response Under More General Conditions
nearby_text: If alleles are completely recessive, allelic effects are small, and the distribution of mutational effects is symmetric, the asymptotic response is approximately $$ r_{m}(\infty)\simeq2N_{e}\bar{\imath}\mu E\left[\alpha^{2}\right]/\sigma_{z} $$ (26.36a)

label: Formula 26.37
section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
subsection: Optimizing the Asymptotic Selection Response
nearby_text: le QTLs, which amounts to maximizing $ N_{e}s $, where s is the selection coefficient on the QTL. For an additive trait, Hill (1985) and Caballero et al. (1996) generalized Equation 25.4 to show that $$ s=\left(\overline{\imath}\frac{a}{\sigma_{z}}\right)\frac{\rho(x,A)}{h} $$ (26.37)
